topic: Area of Study 6: Innovations in Music 1900 to Present
subject: music

items.q: Did music after 1900 abandon all earlier tradition?
items.correct: No - many innovators reworked the past: neoclassicism revived eighteenth-century forms, and serialists wrote dance suites and variations.
items.misconception: Twentieth-century composers rejected everything that came before them.
items.random_wrong: After 1900 composers were forbidden to write for the orchestra.
items.why: Innovation often means transforming inherited material; recognising the dialogue with tradition is central to understanding works from Stravinsky to the present.

items.q: Is musique concrete the same as synthesised electronic music?
items.correct: No - musique concrete manipulates recordings of real-world sounds, whereas elektronische Musik generates sounds electronically from scratch.
items.misconception: All studio-made music of the 1950s is the same thing: electronic music.
items.random_wrong: Musique concrete means music performed on concrete percussion blocks.
items.why: The two Parisian and Cologne schools started from opposite materials - recorded reality versus pure synthesis - a distinction examiners expect students to draw.

items.q: Does aleatoric music let performers do absolutely anything?
items.correct: No - the composer fixes a framework and leaves specific elements, such as the order of fragments, to chance or choice.
items.misconception: In aleatoric music the performers ignore the score and play freely.
items.random_wrong: Aleatoric music is created by an audience vote during the concert.
items.why: Lutoslawski's 'limited aleatoricism' shows the principle: freedom operates only inside boundaries the composer designs, so the result is controlled, not chaotic.
The answer examiners want is: Schoenberg. “Stravinsky” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which composer is most associated with minimalist works such as 'Music for 18 Musicians'?
items.why: Correct answer: Reich. “Glass” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: A cadence ending on chord V, creating a sense of incompleteness.
items.random_wrong: A cadence that sounds wrong or is performed badly.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A cadence ending on chord V, creating a sense of incompleteness.. “Slow.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does 'piano' (p) mean as a dynamic marking?
items.why: It's Quiet/soft.. “The instrument piano.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: Three sections: A, contrasting B, and a return to A (ABA).
items.why: It's Three sections: A, contrasting B, and a return to A (ABA).. “Music in 3/4 time.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “Spectralism”?
items.correct: Late twentieth-century approach analysing the acoustic spectrum of sound, developed by Grisey and Murail in 1970s France.
items.misconception: Music incorporating chance elements, associated with John Cage's '4'33"' (1952) and Lutoslawski.
items.random_wrong: Style emerging in the 1960s using repetition and gradual process, exemplified by Steve Reich and Philip Glass.
items.why: It's Late twentieth-century approach analysing the acoustic spectrum of sound, developed by Grisey and Murail in 1970s France.. “Music incorporating chance elements, associated with John Cage's '4'33"' (1952) and Lutoslawski.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “Atonality”?
items.correct: Music without a tonal centre, pioneered by Schoenberg around 1908 in works such as the Three Piano Pieces Op. 11.
items.misconception: Style using recorded environmental and instrumental sounds as raw material, founded by Pierre Schaeffer in 1948.
items.random_wrong: Style emerging in the 1960s using repetition and gradual process, exemplified by Steve Reich and Philip Glass.
items.why: Correct answer: Music without a tonal centre, pioneered by Schoenberg around 1908 in works such as the Three Piano Pieces Op. 11.. “Style using recorded environmental and instrumental sounds as raw material, founded by Pierre Schaeffer in 1948.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.misconception: Late twentieth-century approach analysing the acoustic spectrum of sound, developed by Grisey and Murail in 1970s France.
items.random_wrong: Music without a tonal centre, pioneered by Schoenberg around 1908 in works such as the Three Piano Pieces Op. 11.
items.why: Correct answer: Style using recorded environmental and instrumental sounds as raw material, founded by Pierre Schaeffer in 1948.. “Late twentieth-century approach analysing the acoustic spectrum of sound, developed by Grisey and Murail in 1970s France.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. items.q: Which of these is the best definition of “Aleatoric music”?
items.correct: Music incorporating chance elements, associated with John Cage's '4'33"' (1952) and Lutoslawski.
items.misconception: Style emerging in the 1960s using repetition and gradual process, exemplified by Steve Reich and Philip Glass.
items.random_wrong: Style using recorded environmental and instrumental sounds as raw material, founded by Pierre Schaeffer in 1948.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Music incorporating chance elements, associated with John Cage's '4'33"' (1952) and Lutoslawski.. “Style emerging in the 1960s using repetition and gradual process, exemplified by Steve Reich and Philip Glass.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did John Cage compose his silent work '4'33"'?
items.why: It's 1952. “1968” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
